titleStmt.title: Changes in running economy and attainable maximal oxygen consumption in response to prolonged running: The impact of training status

affiliation.orgName: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science In Sports published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd. Faculty of Education and Arts

analytic.title: Changes in running economy and attainable maximal oxygen consumption in response to prolonged running: The impact of training status

biblStruct.note: Received: 16 January 2024 | Revised: 10 April 2024 | Accepted: 14 April 2024

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents
application.label: b64991a

startPage=-1, endPage=-1, consolidateCitations=0, consolidateHeader=0, consolidateFunders=0, includeRawAffiliations=false, includeRawCitations=true, includeRawCopyrights=false, generateTeiIds=false, generateTeiCoordinates=[], flavor=null

div.p: During prolonged running at moderate-to-high intensity, running economy (RE) deteriorates and attainable maximal oxygen consumption (VO 2max ) decreases.

Whether these changes appear similarly in trained and untrained runners exercising at the same relative intensity is not clear. We recruited 10 trained runners (TR) and 10 active adults (AA), and compared RE and attainable VO 2max before and after 1 h of running at 70% of VO 2max . Submaximal VO 2 increased more (p = 0.019) in AA (0.20 ± 0.13 L min -1 ) than in TR (0.07 ± 0.05 L min -1 ). Attainable VO 2max decreased in AA (-0.21 ± 0.15 L min -1 , p = 0.002), but remained unchanged in TR (-0.05 ± 0.10 L min -1 , p = 0.18). Relative intensity (i.e., VO 2 /attainable VO 2max ), increased more (p = 0.001) in AA (8.3 ± 4.4%) than in TR (2.6 ± 1.9%). These results demonstrate that the ability to resist changes in RE and VO 2max following prolonged running is superior in trained versus untrained runners, when exercising at the same relative intensity.

div.head: | INTRODUCTION
div.p: Maximal oxygen consumption (VO 2max ) and running economy (RE) are well-known predictors of running performance. 1 In combination, these two factors account for as much as 85%-94% of variability in running performance. 2,3 Most commonly, VO 2max and RE are measured in a rested condition, after a short warm up procedure designed to optimize the result. During long-lasting running competitions, however, measurements obtained in a rested condition may not be representative throughout the competition, as the athlete starts to become fatigued. 4 During prolonged running at a fixed velocity, the oxygen cost of running is shown to increase. In a group of highly trained male long distance runners, Xu and Montgomery 5 observed 1.8 and 3.2 mL kg -1 min -1 increase in VO 2 , following 90 min of running at 65% and 80% of VO 2max , respectively. Similarly, in a group of male physical education students, Sproule 6 observed 2.6 and 3.7 mL kg -1 min -1 increase in VO 2 , running 1 h at 70% and 80% of VO 2max , respectively.

The ability to reach VO 2max during running is also compromised following prolonged heavy running exercise. In a group of trained male runners, attainable VO 2max was reduced from 60.0 to 56.3 mL kg -1 min -1 , when measured 30 min after completion of a half-marathon run. 7 In agreement with this, De Vito et al. 8 observed a 5 mL kg -1 min -1 reduction in running VO 2max (from 69 to 64 mL kg -1 min -1 ), immediately after the first two segments of a triathlon (1.5 km swimming + 32 km cycling). Exercising in the heat may further amplify decrements in attainable VO 2max . 9 Importantly, the combination of reduced attainable VO 2max and increased VO 2 at a given velocity, may significantly increase the relative intensity of prolonged steady pace running. 4 Recently, the term physiological resilience (or durability) has been introduced in the literature, to describe an athlete's ability to resist changes in physiological profiling attributes during prolonged exercise. 4,10,11 In a series of studies, Clark et al. [12][13][14] showed that cycling critical power is markedly reduced after 2 h cycling at 60%-65% of VO 2max . The reduction in critical power was accompanied by, but not correlated with, an increased O 2 cost of cycling. Contrary to the aforementioned studies of Dressendorfer 7 and De Vito et al., 8 Clark et al. [12][13][14] observed no change in cycling VO 2max after 2 h of biking, when assessed during 3 min of all-out effort. This discrepancy may rely on inherent differences between running and cycling exercise, however training status of the subjects may also be influential.

Despite being a relatively new area of research, physiological resilience has already been suggested to represent a fourth parameter of endurance exercise physiology, 4 complementing the established model consisting of VO 2max , lactate threshold, and RE. 15 To date, the literature seems to agree that relatively large differences in physiological resilience exist between individuals, 4,10,[12][13][14]16 however, evidence is scarce of which factors that actually determine this resilience. Perhaps most important, it is not clear whether durability is different between trained and untrained subjects exercising at the same relative intensity; that is, if durability is a physiological trait that positively adapts to training, such as VO 2max and RE. In Clark et al., [12][13][14] critical power resilience did not correlate with any baseline parameter of cardiorespiratory fitness. On the other hand, field data from elite cycling suggests that the ability to produce high wattage in a fatigued state is a characteristic that separates professional road cyclists from elite under 23 and junior cyclists. 17 The purpose of the present study was therefore to investigate whether the ability to resist changes in VO 2max and RE is different between trained and untrained runners, when tested before and after 1 h of running at 70% of VO 2max . Specifically, we hypothesized that (1) the ability to resist changes in RE would be superior in trained versus untrained runners, and (2) the ability to resist changes in VO 2max would be superior in trained versus untrained runners. As an inevitable consequence of Hypotheses 1 and 2, we also hypothesized that (3) the relative intensity, that is, VO 2 /attainable VO 2max , would increase more in the untrained than in the trained runners.

div.head: | Subjects
div.p: A total of 20 healthy volunteers; 12 males and 8 females, aged 31 ± 6 years, participated in the study (Table 1). Ten subjects (6 males and 4 females) were trained runners (TR), and 10 subjects (6 males and 4 females) were active young adults (AA). AA reported to participate regularly in recreational sport and strength training, but did not perform endurance training regularly more than two times a week. AA

Trained runners (n = 10) Active adults (n = 10) Men/Women 6/4 6/4 Age, year 32 ± 8 30 ± 2 Body mass, kg 69 ± 8 79 ± 12 # Height, cm 177 ± 7 176 ± 9 VO 2max (mL kg -1 min -1 ) 64.4 ± 6.9 49.8 ± 4.9 ## Personal best at 10 km running (min) 35.2 ± 3.0 NA Men/Women 33.1 ± 1.3/38.3 ± 1.6 NA Years of running, year 9 ± 4 NA Running distance, km week -1 69 ± 15 9 ± 5 ## Other endurance training, h week -1 3.4 ± 2.3 0.8 ± 1.0 ## High-intensity endurance training, sessions week -1 2.3 ± 0.4 0.9 ± 0.5 ## Note: Data are mean ± SD. # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01 significant difference between groups. T A B L E 1 Subject characteristics of trained runners and active young adults. | 3 of 11 UNHJEM were recruited among University students and staff, as well as local recreational sport clubs. TR were recruited through result lists from local running competitions. Male TR had to be able to run 10 km in less than 36 min, and female TR had to be able to run 10 km in less than 40 min. All subjects reported to be familiar with treadmill running. Written informed consent was obtained from all subjects prior to participation, after having received detailed information about the study. The study was approved by the Norwegian Centre for research data (SIKT reg. 570843), and carried out in agreement with the latest revision of the Declaration of Helsinki. The study was conducted in accordance with Nord University's research ethical regulations. The regional ethical committee of health research reviewed the experimental protocol and confirmed that ethical approval for patient research was not required.

div.head: | Experimental design and physiological testing
div.p: Each subject attained 2 days of testing in the physiological laboratories at Nord University. All running was conducted on a calibrated treadmill (Woodway 4Front, Weil am Rhein, Germany). Running was conducted at 1% incline. 18 Temperature in the laboratory was held at 20-22°C. Subjects were not allowed to use running shoes with carbon plates.

2.2.1 | Test day 1 On the first day of testing, subjects started with 5 min selfdetermined easy running, after which warm up blood lactate concentration ([La -] b ) was established, using a portable lactate analyzer (Lactate Scout 4, EKF Diagnostics, UK). Thereafter, subjects conducted a RE and lactate profiling procedure, consisting of three to four work periods of 5-min running. For the first work period, velocity was set to a predicted intensity of ~50% of VO 2max , 3 based on the subjects self-reported or self-predicted race time at 10 km running. For each step the velocity was increased by 1-2 km h -1 , until [La -] b ≥ 4 mmol L -1 , or the rating of perceived exhaustion ≥7 (RPE 1-10). For the last 3 min of each 5-min work period, VO 2 was measured using a Vyntus CPX with mixing chamber mode (Vyaire medical, USA), to determine RE. Between each work period 1-min rest were given, in which a blood sample was drawn to determine [La -] b . After the RE profiling procedure, subjects were given 10 min active rest (walking or easy running on the treadmill). VO 2max was then determined during an incremental stepwise protocol at either 5% or 10% incline, based on the subjects' preference. By educated guessing, the initial velocity was set somewhat below the subjects' predicted lactate threshold at the chosen incline, and velocity was thereafter increased by 1 km h -1 every minute, until exhaustion, typically within 5-9 min. [La -] b was determined 1 min after termination of the test. Heart rate (HR) was measured using a Polar Pacer heart rate monitor (Kempele, Finland). 2.2.2 | Test day 2 Using RE and VO 2max data from test day 1, the velocity corresponding to 70% of VO 2max was estimated. A linear regression was performed using VO 2 and running velocities from the steps in the lactate profiling procedure with [La -] b < 3 mmol L -1 . The point at which this regression line corresponded to 70% of VO 2max was selected as velocity for the 1-h running session. On the second day of testing, subjects started with 8 min progressive easy running, culminating in 2 min running at the predicted 70% of VO 2max velocity; 10 min warm up in total. VO 2 at the predicted 70% of VO 2max velocity was then established during a 5-min reference run at this velocity, followed by 1 min rest to determine [La -] b .

Thereafter, the subject ran 1 h at the same velocity (hereafter referred to as 1 h running session), during which VO 2 was measured every 15 min (i.e., between 12-15, 27-30, 42-45, and 57-60 min). Data from the 5-min reference run is referred to as "min 0" of the 1 h running session. For each of the 3-min assessment windows, the breathing mouthpiece used to measure VO 2 was inserted while running. Outside these assessment windows subjects did not use the mouthpiece, and were allowed to drink water. After 1 h the subject received a 2-min rest, in which [La -] b and body mass were measured. Immediately after this, treadmill incline was set to 5% or 10% (the same incline used at test day 1), and the subject continued into the exact same VO 2max protocol as on test day 1, to assess attainable VO 2max after 1 h of running. One minute after termination of the VO 2max protocol, [La -] b was obtained.

div.head: | Data analysis
div.p: 2.3.1 | Maximal oxygen consumption VO 2 measurements were sampled every 10 s. To determine VO 2max , the average of the six highest consecutive 10-second periods were used. Gas exchange threshold was determined by visually identifying the first disproportionate increase in VCO 2 versus VO 2 . This procedure was assisted by a computerized regression analysis tool for identification of exercise thresholds (SentrySuite software, Vyaire Medical, USA), as described by Beaver, Wasserman and Whipp. 19 At least 30 s at the final velocity UNHJEM of the stepwise protocol had to be completed for this velocity to be considered velocity at the end of VO 2max stepwise protocol. If 30 s was not completed the former velocity was reported. As incline during the stepwise protocol was not identical between the groups (AA: 9 subjects used 5%, 1 subject used 10% incline; TR: 6 subjects used 5%, 4 subjects used 10% incline) this measure can only be used to quantify within subject changes in performance, between rested and fatigued condition.

div.head: | Running economy
div.p: At the submaximal speeds, VO 2 was sampled for a period of 3 min, of which the average of the last 90 s were used for calculation. RE refers to O 2 cost per kg body mass per meter (mL kg -1 m -1 ), and was calculated as the average value from the submaximal speeds with [La -] b < 3 mmol L -1 . VO 2 refers to the submaximal VO 2 measured during the 1 h running session. Energy expenditure (Kcal min -1 ) was estimated by multiplying VO 2 with the caloric equivalent to the corresponding RER value.

div.head: | Blood lactate
div.p: As blood lactate profiling was not a main research question in this study, a cut of at RPE ≥7 was introduced to ensure that the subsequent VO 2max testing was not compromised by exhaustion from the lactate profiling procedure. As a consequence of this the common lactate threshold value of [La -] b = 4 mmol L -1 was not reached in all subjects.
Results from lactate profiling are reported as velocity and % of VO 2max at warm up [La -] b + 1.5 mmol L -1 , 20,21 and at 2 and 3 mmol L -1 [La -] b .

div.head: | Body mass and fluid intake
div.p: Body mass was measured at three occasions during the study. At the start of test day 1, at the start of test day 2, and immediately after termination of 1 h running at 70% of VO 2max . The difference between the two measurements on test day 2, in addition to the magnitude of fluid intake, were used to calculate weight loss during the 1 h running session.

div.head: | Statistical analysis
div.p: Statistical analyses were performed using the software package SPSS version 29 (Chicago, IL, USA). Figures were made using Graph pad prism 10 (San Diego, CA, USA).

Student's independent t-tests were used to examine differences between groups in the rested condition. Paired ttests were used to examine within group differences (from rested to fatigued condition). Independent t-tests were used to assess differences in changes between the groups (change from rested to fatigued condition). The Pearson test for linear regression was used to assess correlations. Significance level was set to p > 0.05. Data reported in tables and text are means ± SD.

div.head: | Baseline differences between trained runners and active adults
div.p: Most of the measured physiological and performance variables reflected superior capacity and performance level in TR compared to AA (Table 2). Relative VO 2max was ~29% higher in TR than in AA (p < 0.001), and RE was 8% better (p < 0.001). Velocity at warm up [La -] b + 1.5 mmol L -1 was ~41% higher in TR than in AA (p < 0.001). % of VO 2max at warm up [La -] b + 1.5 mmol L -1 was not significantly different between the groups (p = 0.22). The % of VO 2max at gas exchange threshold was not different between the groups (p = 0.32).

div.head: | Changes in cost of running
div.p: During 1 h running, VO 2 (L min -1 ) increased more in AA than in TR (AA: 6.9 ± 3.9% vs. TR: 2.3 ± 1.5%, p < 0.01) (Figure 1, Table 3). Also HR (AA: 17.8 ± 8.3% vs. TR: 6.8 ± 3.5%, p < 0.001), VE (AA: 26.8 ± 17.5% vs. TR: 5.5 ± 6.0%, p < 0.01), and BF (AA: 51.8 ± 26.9% vs. TR: 14.1 ± 13.7%, p < 0.001) increased more in AA than in TR (Figure 2).

div.head: | Changes in VO 2max , relative intensity and performance
div.p: VO 2max (L min -1 ) decreased after 1 h running in AA (-5.0 ± 3.4%, p < 0.01), but remained unchanged in TR (-1.2 ± 2.4%, p = 0.17) (Table 4). The change in VO 2max was different between AA and TR (p = 0.011). The running velocity at the final stage of the VO 2max stepwise protocol decreased in AA (-4.6 ± 5.8%, p < 0.05), but remained unchanged in TR (-1.3 ± 3.2%, p = 0.18). In combination, the increase in VO 2 and the reduction in attainable VO 2max , resulted in a larger increase in relative intensity in AA than in TR (AA: 8.3 ± 4.4% vs. TR: 2.6 ± 1.9%, p = 0.001) (Figure 3). Weight loss (AA: 0.66 ± 0.30 kg vs. TR: 0.73 ± 0.28 kg, p = 0.60), and fluid intake (AA: 0.28 ± 0.26 L vs. TR: 0.37 ± 0.25 L, p = 0.46) during 1 h running were not different between AA and TR.

div.head: | Correlations
div.p: Across groups, ∆VO 2 (mL kg -1 min -1 ) correlated with ∆velocity at the end of VO 2max stepwise protocol (R = -0.45, p < 0.05), ∆HR (R = 0.61, p < 0.01), ∆VE (R = 0.64, p > 0.01), ∆BF (R = 0.71, p < 0.01). ∆VO 2max (mL kg -1 min -1 ) correlated with ∆velocity at the end of VO 2max ramp protocol (R = 0.54, p < 0.05), ∆HR (R = -0.63, p < 0.01), ∆VE (R = -0.55, p = 0.01), and ∆BF (R = -0.62, p < 0.01). Relative intensity at the end of 1 h correlated with ∆velocity at the end of the VO 2max ramp protocol (-0.73, p < 0.001), ∆HR (R = 0.67, p < 0.01), ∆VE (R = 0.67, p > 0.01), and ∆BF (R = 0.70, p < 0.01). Within AA, relative intensity at the end of 1 h correlated with ∆velocity at the end of the VO 2max stepwise protocol (R = -0.715, p < 0.05). ∆VO 2 (mL kg -1 min -1 ) correlated with ∆BF (R = 0.62, p < 0.05). ∆VO 2max (L min -1 ) correlated with ∆BF (R = -0.66, p < 0.05), and ∆HR (R = 0.63, p < 0.05).
Within TR, relative intensity at the end of 1 h correlated with ∆velocity at the end of the VO 2max stepwise protocol (-0.735, p < 0.05).

div.head: | DISCUSSION
div.p: This study sought to investigate whether physiological resilience during prolonged running is different between TR and AA. When tested before and after 1 h running at predicted 70% of VO 2max velocity, the main findings were that (1) VO 2 increased more in AA than in TR, (2) attainable VO 2max decreased in AA but not in TR, and (3) the relative intensity (i.e., VO 2 /attainable VO 2max ) increased more in AA than in TR. These results suggest that the ability to resist changes in RE and attainable VO 2max during prolonged running is superior in TR compared to AA. Arguably, physiological responses to prolonged exercise Gas exchange threshold (mL kg

-1 min -1 ) 39.4 ± 4.3 30.8 ± 3.8 ## Gas exchange threshold (% of VO 2max ) 61.2 ± 3.2 62.0 ± 3.9 1 h velocity (km h -1 ) (predicted 70% of VO 2max ) 13.7 ± 1.4 9.7 ± 1.3 ## % of Gas exchange threshold at 1 h velocity 111 ± 8 113 ± 6 Note: Data are mean ± SD. # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01 significant difference between groups. Abbreviations: [La -] b , lactate concentration in blood; RE, running economy; VO 2max , maximal oxygen consumption. should be considered when profiling endurance athletes, as it may influence performance beyond that of VO 2max and RE data obtained in a rested condition.

div.head: | Changes in running economy during 1 h running
div.p: In line with the hypothesis, VO 2 increased more in AA than in TR. This difference was apparent already after 15 min of running, and continued to increase throughout the 1 h running session. In TR, respiratory exchange ratio (RER) decreased from 0.93 to 0.88. This should, at least in theory, increase VO 2 by 1.2%. Thus, approximately half of the 2.3% increase in VO 2 observed in TR was resultant to a change in substrate utilization from carbohydrate to fat.

In AA, RER decreased from 0.93 to 0.91, which should increase VO 2 by 0.5%. In contrast to TR, this can only explain a small fraction of the 6.9% increase in VO 2 , implying that a decrease in mechanical efficiency was more apparent in AA. When energy cost of running was expressed in terms of Kcal min -1 , the increase was more than fourfold in AA compared to TR. The observed changes in RE is within the range of previous reports. In a group of active male students, Sproule 6 reported 2.6 mL kg -1 min -1 increase in VO 2 after 1 h running at 70% of VO 2max . This is similar to the 2.4 mL kg -1 min -1 increase observed in AA following the exact same running protocol. Evidence from cycling experiments involving subjects of similar fitness level is also within the same range (all ~7%), notably with exercise sessions differing slightly in terms of intensity and duration. 14,22 For TR, direct comparisons to experiments applying the exact same protocol are not available; however, Xu and Montgomery 5 observed 1.8 mL kg -1 min -1 increase in VO 2 after 90 min of running at 65% of VO 2max in a group of trained runners (vs. 1.1 mL kg -1 min -1 after 60 min in TR). Observations along the same line have also been reported among trained cyclists, 12 albeit during longer exercise sessions (~4% increase following 2 h cycling at 65% of VO 2max ). In general, changes in RE appear to become larger with higher intensity 5,6 and longer duration of exercise. 6,13 4.foot_1 | Changes in attainable VO 2max after 1 h running AA exhibited a decrease in attainable VO 2max immediately after the 1 h running session, while TR did not. Whether or not rested values of VO 2max are attainable in a fatigued state has been somewhat inconsistent in previous literature, possibly as a result of differences in subject fitness level, exercise modality, testing protocol, and ) 3.12 ± 0.62 3.13 ± 0.62 3.17 ± 0.65 3.20 ± 0.63 3.19 ± 0.63 0.01 ± 0.03 0.05 ± 0.05 0.08 ± 0.07 0.07 ± 0.06 VO 2 (mL kg -1 min -1 ) 44.8 ± 4.9 45.0 ± 5.1 45.5 ± 5.5** 46.0 ± 5.5** 45.9 ± 5.3** 0.2 ± 0.4 0.7 ± 0.7 1.2 ± 1.0 1.1 ± 0.8 Kcal min -1 15.5 ± 3.0 15.5 ± 3.1 15.6 ± 3.2 15.7 ± 3.1* 15.7 ± 3.1* 0.0 ± 0.2 0.1 ± 0.2 0.3 ± 0.3 0.2 ± 0.3 RER 0.93 ± 0.03 0.92 ± 0.03 0.90 ± 0.02** 0.90 ± 0.02** 0.88 ± 0.02** -0.01 ± 0.02 -0.03 ± 0.01 -0.03 ± 0.02 -0.05 ± 0.03 [La - ] b 1.4 ± 0.5 ---1.4 ± 0.8 ---0.0 ± 0.8 Active adults VO 2 (L min -1 ) 2.67 ± 0.48 2.77 ± 0.54** 2.81 ± 0.56** 2.83 ± 0.59** 2.87 ± 0.59** 0.10 ± 0.08 ## 0.14 ± 0.11 # 0.16 ± 0.12 0.20 ± 0.13 # VO 2 (mL kg -1 min -1 ) 34.0 ± 3.1 35.2 ± 3.5** 35.8 ± 3.5** 36.0 ± 4.0** 36.4 ± 4.0** 1.2 ± 0.9 ## 1.7 ± 1.3 # 2.0 ± 1.4 2.4 ± 1.4 # Kcal min -1 13.2 ± 2.4 13.7 ± 2.7** 13.9 ± 2.8** 14.0 ± 3.0** 14.1 ± 3.0** 0.5 ± 0.4 ## 0.7 ± 0.5 ## 0.8 ± 0.6 # 0.9 ± 0.6 ## RER 0.93 ± 0.03 0.93 ± 0.03 0.92 ± 0.03 0.91 ± 0.04 0.91 ± 0.04 0.00 ± 0.02 -0.01 ± 0.02 # -0.02 ± 0.03 -0.02 ± 0.04 [La - ] b 2.2 ± 0.7 ---2.5 ± 0.7 ---0.3 ± 0.8 Note: 0 min refers to the last 90 s of the 5-min reference run prior to the 1 h running session. *p < 0.05. **p < 0.01 significant difference within group from rested condition to after 1 h running # p < 0.05. ## p < 0.01 significant difference between groups. Abbreviations: [La - ] b , lactate concentration in blood; RER, respiratory exchange ratio; VO 20 30 40 50 Breathing frequency (br • min -1 ) 15 30 45 60 0 ** ** ** ** ** * * # ## ## (A) (B) (C) (D) Rested condition After 1 h ∆ 1 h ∆% 1 h Trained runners (n = 10) VO 2max (L min -1 ) 4.47 ± 0.84 4.41 ± 0.84 -0.05 ± 0.11 -1.2 ± 2.4 VO 2max (mL kg -1 min -1 ) 64.4 ± 6.9 63.7 ± 6.8 -0.8 ± 1.6 -1.2 ± 2.5 HR max (beats min -1 ) 182 ± 7 181 ± 8 -1 ± 2 -0.5 ± 1.2 VE max (L min -1 ) 157 ± 34 155 ± 33 -2 ± 6 -1.3 ± 3.6 RER max 1.14 ± 0.04 1.11 ± 0.02** -0.03 ± 0.03 -2.9 ± 2.6 [La -] b max 12.2 ± 2.6 10.9 ± 3.2 -1.3 ± 2.6 -10.0 ± 21.2 Velocity max (km h -1 ) 16.5 ± 1.7 16.3 ± 1.4 -0.2 ± 0.5 -1.3 ± 3.2 Active adults (n = 10) VO 2max (L min -1 ) 3.93 ± 0.80 3.72 ± 0.69** -0.21 ± 0.15 # -5.0 ± 3.5 ## VO 2max (mL kg -1 min -1 ) 49.8 ± 5.3 47.4 ± 4.2** -2.4 ± 1.8 # -4.6 ± 3.5 # HR max (beats min -1 ) 189 ± 6 192 ± 7 3 ± 4 # 1.4 ± 2.1 # VE max (L min -1 ) 146 ± 32 143 ± 32 -2 ± 8 -1.7 ± 5.0 RER max 1.17 ± 0.03 1.11 ± 0.05** -0.05 ± 0.04 -4.5 ± 3.8 [La -] b max 12.3 ± 2.3 10.4 ± 2.0** -1.9 ± 1.7 -14.3 ± 11.7 Velocity max (km h -1 ) 13.2 ± 1.0 12.6 ± 0.6* -0.7 ± 0.7 -4.6 ± 5.9 Note: Data are mean ± SD. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 significant difference within group from rested condition to after 1 h running. # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01 significant difference between groups. Abbreviations: [La -] b , lactate concentration in blood; HR, heart rate; RER, respiratory exchange ratio; VE, ventilation; Velocity max , velocity at the end of the VO 2max protocol; VO 2max , maximal oxygen consumption.

div.head: T A B L E 4
div.p: Ventilatory and performance data from incremental ramp protocols performed in a rested condition, and 2 min after 1 h of running.

UNHJEM degree of exhaustion. In endurance athletes of approximately the same level as TR, Dressendorfer 7 and De Vito et al. 8 observed 4%-7% decrements in attainable running VO 2max , 30 min after completion of a half marathon 7 and immediately after the first two segments of an Olympic triathlon. 8 While TR did not exhibit such a decrement, the fatiguing exercise bout in Dressendorfer and DeVito et al. were of longer duration and higher intensity than in current study, which likely explains the discrepancy.

In contrast to the studies by Dressendorfer and DeVito et al., Clark et al. [12][13][14] observed no differences in cycling VO 2max , when tested during 3 min of all-out effort, before and after 2 h of cycling at approximately 65% of VO 2max .

In these studies, when VO 2max was assessed by longer continuous effort above critical power, it was lower in the fatigued state, indicating that a short and vigorous testing protocol may be required to reach maximal values in the fatigued state. Given the distinct difference between TR and AA in the current study, comparisons to the Clark studies are not straightforward, as the subjects in two of these three studies were trained cyclists 12 and athletes. 13 Interestingly, in the third study by Clark et al., 14 nonathlete male subjects, comparable to AA, attained similar cycling VO 2max after 2 h of cycling. This raises the question whether prolonged running may lead to larger drift in physiological characteristics than prolonged cycling. 4 Arguably, the repetitive foot strikes during running may have larger impact on the working musculature, possibly leading to muscle damage, 23 especially in subjects that are not used to prolonged running, such as AA. Exercising in the heat (35°C), Wingo, Stone and Ng 9 observed similar decrements in VO 2max after 45 min of running and cycling at 60% of VO 2max , advocating that this may not be the case. It is nevertheless possible that testing modality may influence "VO 2max resilience," as whole-body VO 2max is lower (~6%-9%) when tested during cycling than during running, 24,25 unless subjects are trained cyclists. Running VO 2max , which more accurately represents the upper limit of whole body VO 2max , may therefore be compromised at a lower degree of fatigue than cycling VO 2max , in particular if central factors that compromise cardiac output are subject to fatigue.

div.head: | Changes in relative intensity and performance during 1 h running
div.p: The combined changes in RE and VO 2max resulted in a larger increase in relative intensity in AA than in TR. This difference was mirrored by (and correlated with) a larger increase in HR in AA than in TR, during the 1 h running session. Wingo, Lafrenz, Ganio, Edwards, and Cureton 26 reported that the magnitude of cardiovascular drift during prolonged cycling was related to the magnitude of changes in VO 2max and relative intensity, albeit in a heated environment. Form a practical point of view, if HR monitoring during steady pace exercise has potential to predict changes in VO 2max and RE, it is possible that some primitive assessment of resilience could be made available outside advanced testing laboratories. To the authors best knowledge, this study is the first to show that that the magnitude of cardiovascular drift may be related to training status. It is likely that the difference in ∆HR is related to superior ability to resist changes in VO 2max and RE in TR compared to AA. Interestingly, the change in relative intensity was not only different between TR and AA; also within the groups large differences were observed. Among AA the increase in relative intensity ranged from 1% to 14%, while TR ranged from 0% to 5%. Strikingly, one of the subjects in AA exhibited similar resilience as the best TR subjects, despite having the lowest VO 2max in the study (~44 mL kg -1 min -1 ). This issue is highlighted by Jones,4 reporting that the fatigue-induced change in critical power in the three studies by Clark et al. [12][13][14] ranged from 0.4% to 32% between the subjects, with no correlation to VO 2max . The great interindividual differences in resilience, even within quite homogenous groups, illustrate that the determinants of physiological resilience are not fully understood, and also confirms resilience as an independent physiological variable, as suggested by Jones. 4 In the current study, velocity at the end of the VO 2max stepwise protocol was the only measure of running performance. In agreement with the physiological resilience data, AA terminated the VO 2max protocol at a lower velocity after 1 h running, while TR exhibited no change in performance. Although recognizing that this measure of performance is not the most sensitive, the strong correlation between relative intensity at the end of 1 h running, and change in running performance, demonstrates 1 2 65 70 75 80 % of VO 2max TR AA before after

** ## ** 16000838, 2024, 5, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/sms.14637 by <Shibboleth> member@tudublin.ie, Wiley Online Library on [10/08/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License | 9 of 11 UNHJEM the performance-wise significance of fatigue-induced changes in VO 2max and RE. In agreement with our observations, Clark et al., [12][13][14] reported that % of VO 2max at the end of the fatiguing exercise session was the single variable that correlated strongest with fatigue-induced change in performance during 3 min all-out cycling. Perhaps unsurprising, avoiding to drift into very high relative intensity during prolonged steady pace exercise, appears to conserve performance during a few minutes of all out exercise, such as for example the last kilometer of a marathon.

div.head: | Determinants of physiological resilience
div.p: Although TR exhibited superior physiological resilience compared to AA, the determinants of this difference are difficult to deduct from the current data. During the 1 h running session, [La -] b was somewhat higher in AA than in TR. While this difference could suggest that AA were exercising in a higher intensity domain than TR, the difference in [La -] b was present also during the easy warm up procedures. An increase in [La -] b above warm up levels was apparent at "min 0" in both TR and AA (p < 0.01 in both groups), and the magnitude of increase was not different between the groups (TR: 0.7 ± 0.5 mmol L -1 ; AA: 0.9 ± 0.7 mmol L -1 , p = 0.38). Indeed, lower [La -] b are observed at metabolic thresholds with increasing aerobic capacity. 27 In agreement with this interpretation, gas exchange threshold occurred at similar percentage of VO 2max in the two groups, and below 70% of VO 2max in all subjects, advocating that all subjects exercised in the heavy-intensity domain from the beginning of the 1 h running session. Exercising in the heavy-intensity domain is associated with a VO 2 slow component, leading to a slight increase in VO 2 over time. 28 Indeed, such an increase was observed in both TR and AA, but the magnitude of increase was larger in AA. With regard to the upper end of the heavyintensity domain, the lack of change in [La -] b during 1 h running indicates that both TR and AA were below maximal lactate steady state 29,30 and critical speed. 31 However, it is still possible that AA were closer to the upper boundary of the heavy-intensity domain than TR, as could be indicated by the lower percentage of VO 2max at 2 and 3 mmol L -1 [La-] b observed in AA. Even when percentage of VO 2max was compared at warm up [La-] b + 1.5 mmol L -1 , which is probably a more fair comparison between the groups, a nonsignificant ~2% difference was apparent in favor of TR. For sure, VE, BF, HR, and RPE development during the 1 h running session indicate that TR were closer to physiological steady state than AA. Based on weight loss and fluid intake, dehydration was similar between groups. Carbohydrate availability has been introduced as a possible determinant of physiological resilience. 4,13 In the current study no significant differences were observed between groups in RER development throughout the 1 h running session, or at the end of the VO 2max protocols. Both groups exhibited a decrease in [La -] b max after the incremental stepwise protocol in the fatigued condition, which is likely to reflect glycogen depletion. However, this decrease was not different between the groups.

Changes in VE, BF, and HR correlated with changes in VO 2 , VO 2max and relative intensity, and were considerably larger in AA than in TR (∆HR 18% vs. 7%, ∆VE 28% vs. 5%, ∆BF 50% vs. 11%). While the O 2 -cost of increased HR is limited, the increased ventilation has larger potential to impact VO 2 , and may possibly explain part of the changes in VO 2 during 1 h running, especially in AA. Although the increase in ventilation is likely secondary to an increased metabolic demand of running, the combination of decreased mechanical efficiency and a corresponding increase in ventilation may amplify the increase in VO 2 during prolonged exercise in subjects with poor resilience.

Given the lack of relation to any obvious physiological determinant, the most likely explanation of the difference in resilience between TR and AA remains the exposure to systematic running training. In the current study a weekly training load of 69 km running + 3.4 h other endurance training was associated with superior resilience than 9 km running + 0.8 h other endurance training. Field data from elite cyclists indicate that resilience may continue to increase up to the highest levels, with even more extensive and long-term exposure to endurance training. 17 On the lower end of the scale, the magnitude and type of training required to induce improvements in physiological resilience have yet to be explored, beyond the fact that the training exposure of TR in the current study appears to be sufficient to improve resilience.

div.head: | Study limitations
div.p: The somewhat "old school" approach used to normalize exercise intensity between subjects in the current study, using a fixed % of VO 2max , has been problematized in recent literature. 32,33 Specifically, this method does not take into account that the boundaries between different intensity domains (i.e., moderate, heavy, and severe) occur at different % of VO 2max between subjects. As a consequence of this, physiological responses to a given % of VO 2max may be quite heterogeneous, especially if subjects are exercising in different intensity domains. Although gas exchange threshold data indicates that 70% of VO 2max was in the heavy domain for all subjects, it cannot be excluded that AA were closer to their maximal lactate steady state than TR. In turn, such higher metabolic strain in AA may explain some of the observed differences in resilience between AA and TR.

div.head: | CONCLUSION
div.p: During 1 h running at 70% of VO 2max , VO 2 increased more in AA than in TR, while attainable VO 2max decreased in AA and remained unchanged in TR. As a consequence of this, relative intensity increased more in AA than in TR. These findings imply that TR exhibit superior ability to resist changes in RE and VO 2max compared to AA, which may indicate that long term systematic running training increases physiological resilience.

div.head: | Perspective
div.p: For decades, the three-headed model consisting of maximal oxygen consumption, lactate threshold, and work economy 15 has constituted the foundation of endurance physiology research. Arguably, as suggested by Jones, 4 physiological resilience may compliment this model as a fourth parameter, as the three original components are susceptible to change during prolonged exercise. The fact that TR are superior in resisting changes in VO 2max and RE compared to AA, may indicate that physiological resilience is a trait that adapts to training, although longitudinal experiments are required to confirm this. Being a relatively new area of research, knowledge of the factors that influences physiological resilience are sparse. Knowledge of which training exposure, or for that matter factors other than training that influences physiological resilience, would be useful for anyone striving to improve endurance performance.

figure.head: T A B L E 2 10 )VO 2 ( 1 53

figure.head: F I G U R E 1
figure.figDesc: (A) Change in oxygen cost of running at predicted 70% of VO 2max velocity, from the beginning to the end of 1 h running. (B) Change in attainable VO 2max from the rested condition to 2 min after the end of 1 h running. Data are mean ± SD. n = 10 in both groups. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 change within group from rested condition to the end of 1 h running. # p < 0.05 magnitude of change is different between groups.

figure.head: T A B L E 3
figure.figDesc: Data from 1 h running at the velocity corresponding to 70% of VO 2max .

figure.head: F I G U R E 3
figure.figDesc: Change in relative intensity (submaximal VO 2 / attainable VO 2max ) from rested condition to the end of 1 h running. Data are mean ± SD. n = 10 in both groups. **p < 0.01 change within group from rested condition to the end of 1 h running. ## p < 0.01 magnitude of change is different between groups.

div.head: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
div.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author. The data are not publicly available due to privacy or ethical restrictions.

div.head: ORCID
div.p: Runar Jakobsen Unhjem https://orcid. org/0000-0002-4588-3711

div.head: FUNDING INFORMATION
div.p: No funding was recieved for this study.

div.head: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
div.p: The author reports no conflict of interest.
